questions.question_text: A seller lists a three-unit residential building in Cook County. The seller's agent asks whether a disclosure report is required. Under the Residential Real Property Disclosure Act, which statement is correct?
questions.correct_answer: The seller must provide the disclosure report
distractors: Only single-family homes require disclosure
Properties with more than two units are exempt
The agent completes the report for the seller
questions.explanation: The Illinois Residential Real Property Disclosure Act (765 ILCS 77) applies to residential property of one to four units. A three-unit building falls within that range, so the seller must provide the Residential Real Property Disclosure Report.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives a Residential Real Property Disclosure Report stating the seller has no knowledge of roof leaks. After closing, the buyer discovers long-standing water damage with prior repair receipts in the seller's name. What is the seller's potential liability?
questions.correct_answer: Liability for intentional misrepresentation on the report
distractors: No liability because the report is not a warranty
Liability only if the agent also knew about leaks
No liability because the sale already closed
questions.explanation: The disclosure report reflects the seller's actual knowledge and is not a warranty, but that does not shield a seller who lies. Under 765 ILCS 77/25 the seller must disclose material defects actually known, and under 765 ILCS 77/55 a seller who discloses information on the report that the seller knows to be false is liable for actual damages and court costs, and the court may award attorney's fees. Repair receipts in the seller's name show the seller had actual knowledge of the roof problem and misrepresented it.

questions.question_text: Under the Residential Real Property Disclosure Act, when must the seller deliver the Residential Real Property Disclosure Report to a prospective buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Before the buyer and seller sign the purchase contract
distractors: Within five days after the closing date
At the final walkthrough before closing
Only when the buyer's lender requests it
questions.explanation: Section 20 of the Act (765 ILCS 77/20) requires the seller to deliver the completed disclosure report to the prospective buyer before the signing of the contract. If a report disclosing a material defect is instead delivered after the parties have signed, the buyer may terminate the contract within 5 business days after receipt (765 ILCS 77/40). In practice, Illinois attorneys also evaluate the report during attorney review.

questions.question_text: A seller agrees to sell a duplex 'as-is' and tells the listing agent that no disclosure report is needed because of the as-is clause. How should the agent respond?
questions.correct_answer: The seller must still provide the disclosure report
distractors: An as-is sale eliminates the disclosure requirement
Only a verbal disclosure is needed for as-is sales
The agent may sign the report on the seller's behalf
questions.explanation: In Illinois, selling property as-is does not eliminate the seller's obligation to provide the Residential Real Property Disclosure Report. The disclosure duty under 765 ILCS 77 applies regardless of any as-is contract language.

questions.question_text: A seller fails to provide the required Residential Real Property Disclosure Report before the contract is signed. What remedy is available to the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer may rescind the contract
distractors: The buyer must proceed and sue after closing
The contract is automatically void at closing
The buyer can only request a price reduction
questions.explanation: If the seller does not provide the Residential Real Property Disclosure Report as required, the buyer has the right to rescind the contract. This is a statutory remedy designed to ensure buyers receive material property information before committing to a purchase.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks a listing agent whether anyone died in the home. Under Illinois law, the agent should:
questions.correct_answer: Inform the buyer that this is not a required disclosure
distractors: Disclose the death if it occurred within five years
Provide a written death history for the property
Refuse to answer and refer the buyer to the seller
questions.explanation: Illinois law provides that sellers and agents are not required to disclose that a property was the site of a death, felony, or suicide. The agent cannot be held liable for failing to disclose these stigmatizing events.

questions.question_text: A prospective buyer learns a previous occupant of a home had HIV/AIDS and asks the seller's agent to confirm. Under Illinois disclosure law, what is the agent's obligation?
questions.correct_answer: The agent has no duty to disclose this information
distractors: The agent must disclose if directly asked by a buyer
The agent must confirm and note it on the disclosure form
The agent must disclose if the occupant still lives nearby
questions.explanation: Illinois law specifically protects sellers and agents from liability for not disclosing that an occupant had HIV or AIDS. This information is considered a stigmatizing factor, not a physical defect, and disclosure is not required even when asked directly.

questions.question_text: A home was the site of a violent felony two years ago. The property has no resulting physical damage. Which statement best describes the seller's disclosure duty in Illinois?
questions.correct_answer: The seller is not required to disclose the felony
distractors: The seller must disclose felonies within three years
The seller must disclose only if buyers ask directly
The seller must note it on the disclosure report
questions.explanation: Under Illinois stigmatized property law, the seller is not required to disclose that a felony occurred on the property. The protection applies to events that psychologically stigmatize property, not to physical defects. If there were physical damage, that would need to be disclosed separately.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent is concerned about a property's history as a possible crime scene. The agent advises the buyer that under Illinois law, stigmatized property information:
questions.correct_answer: Must be independently investigated by the buyer
distractors: Will appear on the seller's disclosure report
Must be disclosed by the listing agent if known
Can be obtained from county death records on request
questions.explanation: Illinois law does not require sellers or agents to disclose stigmatizing events such as crimes, deaths, or suicides. If a buyer wants this information, the buyer must conduct independent research. Agents are shielded from liability for not volunteering it.

questions.question_text: An agent listed a home where a suicide occurred last year. A buyer later sues the agent for not disclosing this fact. What is the likely outcome?
questions.correct_answer: The agent is protected from liability by statute
distractors: The agent is liable for concealing material facts
The agent must pay damages for emotional distress
The agent is liable only if the suicide was publicized
questions.explanation: Illinois law expressly provides that agents cannot be held liable for failing to disclose that a property was the site of a suicide. This statutory protection applies regardless of whether the event was publicly known.

questions.question_text: A licensee is preparing a transaction for a home built in 1965. Which federal disclosure requirement applies to this sale?
questions.correct_answer: Lead-based paint disclosure with EPA pamphlet
distractors: Asbestos inspection report from a certified lab
Mold remediation certificate from the seller
Energy efficiency audit from a licensed inspector
questions.explanation: Federal law requires lead-based paint disclosure for all residential properties built before 1978. The seller must provide the EPA pamphlet and allow the buyer a 10-day period to conduct a lead inspection. This applies in Illinois as in all states.

questions.question_text: A home in central Illinois is under contract negotiations. Under the Illinois Radon Awareness Act, before the buyer is obligated under the contract, the seller must:
questions.correct_answer: Provide the buyer with the IEMA radon pamphlet and radon disclosure form
distractors: Order a radon test before the contract is signed
Certify that radon levels are below 4 pCi/L
Include radon test results in the listing data
questions.explanation: The Illinois Radon Awareness Act (420 ILCS 46/10) requires the seller, not the licensee, to give the buyer the IEMA pamphlet 'Radon Testing Guidelines for Real Estate Transactions' and the Illinois Disclosure of Information on Radon Hazards form before the buyer is obligated under the contract. Much of central and northern Illinois is in EPA Zone 1, the highest radon-potential category, but the Act requires disclosure only; it does not require the seller to test, mitigate, or certify radon levels.

questions.question_text: A property is being transferred through a foreclosure sale in Illinois. Which disclosure obligation applies?
questions.correct_answer: The transfer is exempt from the disclosure act
distractors: The lender must complete the seller's disclosure report
The foreclosing court must order a property inspection
Standard residential disclosure rules apply to the lender
questions.explanation: Foreclosure-related transfers, such as a judicial deed issued pursuant to a foreclosure sale or a deed in lieu of foreclosure, are exempt under 765 ILCS 77/15. Other exemptions include court-ordered transfers (including by a bankruptcy trustee), transfers by fiduciaries administering an estate, guardianship, or trust, transfers between co-owners, transfers to a spouse or lineal relatives, transfers to or from governmental entities, and newly constructed homes that have never been occupied.

questions.question_text: A licensee showing a property notices a large crack in the basement wall but the seller's disclosure report says 'no knowledge of structural issues.' What is the agent's duty?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the observed defect to prospective buyers
distractors: Rely solely on the seller's completed disclosure report
Recommend the seller amend the report after closing
Ignore the crack unless a home inspector flags it
questions.explanation: Under Article 15 of the Real Estate License Act (225 ILCS 454/15-25), a licensee must treat customers honestly, must not knowingly or negligently give false information, and must timely disclose latent material adverse facts about the property's physical condition that the licensee actually knows. Having personally observed the crack, the agent has actual knowledge of a material defect and cannot rely on, or continue distributing, a seller disclosure report the agent knows is inaccurate.

questions.question_text: Under federal lead-based paint disclosure rules, how many days must a buyer be given to conduct a lead inspection on a pre-1978 home in Illinois?
questions.correct_answer: 10 days unless the buyer waives the right
distractors: 5 business days with no option to waive
14 calendar days as required by Illinois law
30 days as part of the standard inspection period
questions.explanation: Federal lead-based paint disclosure rules provide the buyer with a 10-day period to conduct a lead-based paint inspection or risk assessment. The buyer may waive this right but cannot be forced to do so. This is a federal requirement that applies uniformly across all states.
